questions.to_find: The correct expressions for boxes B1 and B2 in the height computation pseudo-code

understand.plain: We need to fill in the missing return expressions in a recursive function that computes the height of a binary tree. Height is the number of edges on the longest root-to-leaf path. For an internal node, the height equals 1 plus the maximum of the heights of its left and right subtrees.

keywords.explain: The number of edges on the longest path from root to any leaf node
keywords.example: A single node has height 0; a root with one child has height 1

keywords.explain: Height of a node = 1 + max(height of left subtree, height of right subtree)
keywords.example: If left subtree height = 2 and right subtree height = 3, node height = 1 + 3 = 4

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="320" height="180" viewBox="0 0 320 180" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx="160" cy="30" r="20" class="hi"/><text x="160" y="35" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">A</text><line x1="145" y1="48" x2="100" y2="88" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="175" y1="48" x2="220" y2="88" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="100" cy="108" r="20" class="bg"/><text x="100" y="113" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">B</text><circle cx="220" cy="108" r="20" class="bg"/><text x="220" y="113" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">C</text><line x1="100" y1="128" x2="70" y2="155" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="70" cy="165" r="12" class="bg"/><text x="70" y="169" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">D</text><text x="160" y="170" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">height(A) = 1 + max(h(B), h(C)) = 2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Binary tree showing root A with left child B and right child C; B has a leaf child D. Height is computed recursively.

given.aim: Identify the correct return expression for an internal node in a recursive height function

terms.meaning: Leaf node check; returns 0 for leaves (0 edges)
terms.example: A node with no children has height 0

terms.meaning: Recursive height of the left subtree
terms.example: If left child is a leaf, h1 = 0
terms.connects: One of two subproblem results to combine

terms.meaning: Recursive height of the right subtree
terms.example: If right subtree has 2 levels, h2 = 2
terms.connects: Other subproblem result; combined with h1 to compute node height

given.plan: For any internal node, height = 1 + max(left subtree height, right subtree height). The code already computes h1 and h2, so B1 (and B2, the general case) should both be 1 + max(h1, h2).

steps.title: Understand what B1 should return
steps.apply: When a node has at least one non-NULL child, the height is 1 (for the edge to the child) plus the maximum height among both subtrees. h1 and h2 have already been computed, so B1 = 1 + max(h1, h2).
steps.note: The 'else' branch handles all nodes that are NOT NULL and NOT leaves, so both children may or may not be NULL — but h1 and h2 handle NULL via the base case returning -1.

steps.title: Verify option B is correct
steps.apply: Looking at option B: B1 = (1 + max(h1, h2)) and B2 = (1 + max(h1, h2)). Both boxes represent the same return value for internal nodes. The code structure in the problem has a single return point (B1) so B2 here is the correct label for that same expression. Option A uses height(n->right) directly (redundant call), C and D use max without +1. Only B uses the pre-computed h1, h2 with correct +1.
steps.note: Using max(h1, h2) without +1 would give height of subtree, not height through this node.

questions.formula_note: Height of binary tree node = 1 + max(height of left subtree, height of right subtree); base case: NULL returns -1, leaf returns 0.

questions.question: Consider a rooted n node binary tree represented using pointers. The best upper bound on the time required to determine the number of subtrees having exactly 4 nodes is O(n^a * log^b(n)). Then the value of a + 10b is __________.
questions.to_find: The value of a + 10b where O(n^a * log^b(n)) is the best upper bound on time to count subtrees with exactly 4 nodes

understand.plain: We have a binary tree with n nodes and want to count how many subtrees have exactly 4 nodes each. We need to figure out the time complexity of the best algorithm for this task, expressed as O(n^a * log^b(n)), then compute a + 10b.

keywords.explain: The size (number of nodes) of each subtree can be computed via a single post-order traversal, visiting each node once and summing children counts
Visit left subtree, right subtree, then process current node; allows subtree sizes to be computed as children results are available
keywords.example: For counting subtree sizes: first compute sizes of both children, then add 1 for current node

understand.visual_alt: A rooted binary tree where each node's subtree size is computed bottom-up; nodes with size 4 are counted.

given.aim: Determine the time complexity of counting subtrees with exactly 4 nodes in an n-node binary tree

terms.meaning: Total number of nodes in the binary tree
terms.example: n = 15 for a complete binary tree of height 3
terms.connects: Determines the size of the input and bounds the traversal time

terms.meaning: The time complexity parameterized by a and b
terms.example: O(n) corresponds to a=1, b=0; O(n log n) corresponds to a=1, b=1
terms.connects: We need to identify a and b from the actual complexity, then compute a + 10b

terms.meaning: A connected subgraph rooted at some node v where the total node count (v + all descendants) equals 4
terms.example: A node with two children each having one child: that node's subtree has 4 nodes
terms.connects: The target condition to check for each node

given.plan: Perform a single post-order traversal of the tree. For each node, compute the subtree size as 1 + size(left child) + size(right child). Count how many subtrees have size exactly 4. This traversal visits each node exactly once, taking O(n) time overall. So a=1, b=0, giving a + 10b = 1 + 0 = 1.

steps.title: Design the optimal algorithm
steps.apply: Use a single post-order traversal. At each node v, compute the subtree size recursively. Since each node is visited exactly once and O(1) work is done per node, total time = O(n).
steps.note: No sorting, hashing, or repeated traversal is needed. One pass suffices.

steps.title: Identify a and b, then compute a + 10b
steps.apply: The best upper bound is O(n), meaning a = 1 and b = 0. Therefore a + 10b = 1 + 10*0 = 1.
steps.note: Any algorithm must read all n nodes at least once (lower bound is Omega(n)), so O(n) is tight.

questions.formula_note: Single post-order traversal computes subtree sizes in O(n); counting subtrees of a fixed size is O(n) overall.

questions.question:
The height of a tree is the length of the longest root-to-leaf path in it. The maximum and minimum number of nodes in a binary tree of height 5 are:

A. 63 and 6, respectively
B. 64 and 5, respectively
C. 32 and 6, respectively
D. 31 and 5, respectively
questions.to_find: Maximum and minimum number of nodes in a binary tree of height 5

understand.plain: Height 5 means the longest path from root to a leaf has 5 edges (6 nodes on that path). A maximum binary tree of height h has all levels completely filled (complete binary tree), and a minimum binary tree of height h is just a single chain (path) from root to leaf.

keywords.explain: Length (number of edges) of the longest root-to-leaf path
keywords.example: A path of 5 edges means 6 nodes; height = 5

keywords.explain: Every level is fully filled; maximizes the number of nodes for a given height
keywords.example: Height 2 complete binary tree has 2^3 - 1 = 7 nodes

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="340" height="160" viewBox="0 0 340 160" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x="10" y="20" class="lbl ink">Max nodes (full tree):</text><text x="10" y="40" class="sub mut">Levels 0-5: 1+2+4+8+16+32 = 63</text><text x="10" y="60" class="sub mut">= 2^(h+1) - 1 = 2^6 - 1 = 63</text><text x="10" y="90" class="lbl ink">Min nodes (chain/path):</text><text x="10" y="110" class="sub mut">One node per level: h+1 = 5+1 = 6</text><rect x="5" y="130" width="320" height="25" class="hi" rx="4"/><text x="165" y="147" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">Answer: max=63, min=6</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Max nodes formula 2^(h+1)-1 = 63 for height 5; min nodes = h+1 = 6 for a chain.

given.aim: Find maximum and minimum node counts for a binary tree of height 5

terms.meaning: The height of the binary tree (5 edges on the longest root-to-leaf path)
terms.example: A chain of 6 nodes: root -> n1 -> n2 -> n3 -> n4 -> leaf has height 5
terms.connects: All formulas for max/min are expressed in terms of h

terms.meaning: Achieved by a complete binary tree where every level has the maximum possible nodes
Achieved by a skewed (degenerate/chain) tree with exactly one node at each level
terms.example: Height 3: 4 nodes in a chain = h+1

given.plan: Apply formulas: max_nodes = 2^(h+1) - 1, min_nodes = h + 1, with h = 5.

steps.title: Compute maximum nodes for height 5
steps.note: This is the complete binary tree with all levels fully filled from level 0 to level 5.

steps.title: Compute minimum nodes for height 5
steps.note: A completely skewed (path-like) tree: each node has exactly one child except the leaf. 6 nodes on the path from root to leaf.

solution.result: Maximum = 63 and Minimum = 6, which corresponds to option A.

questions.formula_note: For a binary tree of height h: max nodes = 2^(h+1) - 1 (complete binary tree); min nodes = h+1 (skewed/chain tree).

questions.question: A binary tree T has 20 leaves. The number of nodes in T having two children is ______.
questions.to_find: Number of internal nodes with exactly two children in a binary tree with 20 leaves

understand.plain: In any binary tree, there is a well-known relationship between the number of leaf nodes and the number of nodes with exactly two children. If L is the number of leaves and I_2 is the number of nodes with exactly two children, then I_2 = L - 1. This is a standard theorem for binary trees.

keywords.explain: A node with no children (degree 0 in terms of children)
keywords.example: In a complete binary tree of height 2: the 4 bottom-level nodes are leaves

keywords.explain: A node that has both a left child and a right child (full node)
keywords.example: The root of a complete binary tree of height 2 has two children

understand.visual_alt: Binary tree where leaf count L = 20, and nodes with two children = L - 1 = 19.

given.aim: Use the leaf-to-full-node relationship in binary trees to find the count of two-child nodes

terms.meaning: Number of leaf nodes (nodes with 0 children) in tree T
terms.example: Leaves are the bottom-most nodes with no descendants
terms.connects: Directly determines I_2 via the formula I_2 = L - 1

terms.meaning: Number of nodes with 0 children (same as leaves)
terms.connects: Used in the edge-counting proof of the theorem

terms.meaning: Standard result: in any binary tree (no restriction on structure), nodes with 2 children = leaves - 1
terms.example: A tree with 5 leaves has exactly 4 nodes with 2 children
terms.connects: Direct formula to compute the answer

given.plan: Apply the binary tree theorem: number of nodes with 2 children = (number of leaves) - 1 = 20 - 1 = 19.

steps.title: State and apply the binary tree theorem
steps.formula_raw: I_2 = L - 1  (where L = leaves, I_2 = nodes with two children)
steps.apply: Proof sketch: Let n = total nodes, I_1 = nodes with one child, I_2 = nodes with two children, L = leaves. Edge count = n - 1 = I_1 + 2*I_2 (edges from parents). Also n = L + I_1 + I_2. Substituting: L + I_1 + I_2 - 1 = I_1 + 2*I_2 => L - 1 = I_2.
steps.note: This theorem holds for any binary tree regardless of structure.

steps.title: Substitute L = 20
steps.apply: With 20 leaves, the number of nodes with exactly two children = 20 - 1 = 19.
steps.note: The number of single-child nodes (I_1) can be anything; the formula holds regardless.

questions.formula_note: Binary tree theorem: in any binary tree, number of nodes with exactly two children = number of leaf nodes - 1 (I_2 = L - 1).

questions.question: Consider a binary tree T that has 200 leaf nodes. Then the number of nodes in T that have exactly two children are ______.
questions.to_find: Number of nodes with exactly two children in a binary tree that has 200 leaf nodes

understand.plain: This question is essentially the same as the previous one but with 200 leaves. Using the standard binary tree theorem, the number of nodes with exactly two children equals the number of leaves minus one.

keywords.explain: In any binary tree, the count of nodes with exactly two children is always one less than the count of leaf nodes
A node with no children in the binary tree
keywords.example: In a full binary tree of 7 nodes, there are 4 leaves

understand.visual_alt: A binary tree with 200 leaves. Nodes with two children = 200 - 1 = 199 by the standard binary tree theorem.

given.aim: Apply the binary tree leaf-to-full-node relationship to 200 leaves

terms.meaning: Total number of leaf nodes in binary tree T
terms.connects: Directly plugged into the formula I_2 = L - 1

terms.meaning: The number of nodes with two children in any binary tree equals leaves minus 1
terms.example: For a balanced binary tree with 8 leaves, I_2 = 7
terms.connects: Core formula used to derive the answer

terms.meaning: Total node count equals sum of leaves, single-child nodes, and two-child nodes
terms.example: 7-node complete tree: L=4, I_1=0, I_2=3, n=7
terms.connects: Part of the proof of the theorem

steps.title: Recall the binary tree theorem
steps.apply: For any binary tree: count_of_edges = n - 1. Edges also = I_1 * 1 + I_2 * 2 (each node with 1 child contributes 1 edge down, each node with 2 children contributes 2 edges down). Also n = L + I_1 + I_2. So L + I_1 + I_2 - 1 = I_1 + 2*I_2 => L - 1 = I_2.
steps.note: This proof is completely general — applies to any binary tree shape.

steps.title: Compute the answer for L = 200
steps.note: The answer is 199 regardless of how the tree is structured (balanced, skewed, or arbitrary).

questions.formula_note: Binary tree theorem: in any binary tree, number of nodes with exactly two children = number of leaf nodes - 1 (I_2 = L - 1).
